5: uvaneita sham mizbea'ch l'YHWH Eloheicha mizbea'ch avanim lo-tanif aleihem barzel (ûb̠ānîtā šām mizbēaḥ laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā mizbēaḥ ʾăbānîm lōʾ-tānîp̄ ʿălêhem barzel): 'And you shall build there an altar to the LORD your God, an altar of stones. You shall not wield an iron tool on them.' The altar at Mt. Ebal (the curses-mountain) is to be built from uncut, undressed stones — natural stones untouched by iron tools. The same prohibition appears in Exod 20:25: to wield an iron tool on the stone would defile it (wa-tachallelah). The principle is that YHWH's altar must not be shaped by human craft — the natural roughness of the stone is the appropriate approach to a holy altar. The contrast with Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6:7: no hammer, chisel, or any iron tool was heard in the house) maintains the same principle: the place of divine encounter is not a human achievement but a divine provision. The roughness of Ebal's altar amid the covenant curses may foreshadow the cross — the rough wood of a tree, a place of curse (Deut 21:23), becoming the altar of the covenant.
26: arur asher lo-yakim et-divrei hatorah hazot la'asot otam ve'amar kol-ha'am amen (ʾārûr ʾăšer lōʾ-yāqîm ʾet-dibrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt laʿăśôt ʾôtām wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who does not confirm the words of this law by doing them. And all the people shall say, Amen.' The climactic curse of the Deuteronomic ceremony. Arur (cursed) is a performative declaration: to speak arur over someone is to invoke covenant malediction, the opposite of baruch (blessed). Yakim (confirm, uphold, establish) from qum — to stand/rise; in the Hiphil: to cause to stand, to establish. The requirement is not merely to hear or to intend but to do (la'asot). Paul's citation of this verse in Galatians 3:10 is decisive: all who rely on works of the law are under a curse, for it is written, cursed is everyone who does not abide by all things written in the book of the law and do them. The Deuteronomic curse reveals the law's own logic: it requires total performance, which no one achieves, making it a curse-pronouncing instrument rather than a salvation vehicle. Christ redeems from this curse (Gal 3:13) by himself becoming the arur of Deut 21:23 — cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree.
1: vayetzav Moshe veziqnei Yisrael et-ha'am lemor shamor et-kol-hamitzvah asher anochi metzaveh etchem hayom (wayyĕṣaw mōšê wĕziqnê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-hāʿām lēʾmōr šāmôr ʾet-kol-hammiṣwâ ʾăšer ʾānōḵî mĕṣawwê ʾetḵem hayyôm): Moses and the elders of Israel command the people to keep all the commandment this day. The plural authority — Moses and the elders — signals the beginning of the covenant ratification ceremony that will be performed at Shechem (between Gerizim and Ebal). The ziqnei Yisrael (elders of Israel) act as co-commanders, representing the people's representative leadership structures that will persist after Moses' death. The ceremony is prospective but commanded now — obedience is not waiting for entry but is already demanded.
2: vehayah beyom asher ta'avru et-haYarden el-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lach vahaqimota lecha avanim gedolot vesadta otam beshid (wĕhāyâ b̠ayyôm ʾăšer taʿăb̠rû ʾet-hayyardēn ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā nōtēn lāk wĕhăqîmōtā lĕkā ʾăb̠ānîm gĕdōlôt wĕśadtā ʾôtām b̠aśśîḏ): 'And on the day you cross the Jordan to the land that the LORD your God is giving you, you shall set up large stones and plaster them with plaster.' Avanim gedolot (great/large stones): standing stones as monuments and law-carriers. Sadta otam beshid (plaster them with plaster): the inscribed law is made durable and public — plastered steles were a known ancient practice (cf. the Karnak reliefs and Amarna correspondence). The Jordan crossing is the threshold event; the monument is to be raised on the same day — immediacy signals the covenant's urgency.
3: vekatavta aleihem et-kol-divrei hatorah hazot be'ovrecha lema'an asher tavo el-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lach eretz zavat chalav udvash ka'asher diber YHWH Elohi avoteicha lach (wĕkātab̠tā ʿălêhem ʾet-kol-dibrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt b̠ĕʿob̠rĕkā lĕmaʿan ʾăšer tāb̠ōʾ ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā nōtēn lāk ʾereṣ zāb̠at ḥālāb̠ ûd̠ĕb̠aš kāʾăšer dib̠ber Yhwh ʾĕlōhê ʾăb̠ôtêkā lāk): Write on the stones all the words of this law when you cross over, so that you may enter the land YHWH your God is giving you, a land of milk and honey as he promised. The writing of the law is the precondition for inhabiting the land. Kol-divrei hatorah hazot (all the words of this law): the totality of the covenant document — not a selection but the complete Torah — is to be publicly inscribed. The law is not esoteric or priestly property but a public text accessible to all.
4: vehayah be'ovretchem et-haYarden taqimu et-ha'avanim ha'eleh asher anochi metzaveh etchem hayom behar Eival vesadta otam beshid (wĕhāyâ b̠ĕʿob̠rĕḵem ʾet-hayyardēn tāqîmû ʾet-hāʾăb̠ānîm hāʾēllê ʾăšer ʾānōḵî mĕṣawwê ʾetḵem hayyôm b̠ĕhar ʿêb̠āl wĕśadtā ʾôtām b̠aśśîḏ): Set up these stones on mount Ebal — the mountain of cursing. The choice of Eival (Ebal) for the inscription is theologically provocative: the law is written on the mountain of curses, not the mountain of blessings. This sequence underscores that the Torah's primary function in the fallen human condition is to expose transgression (cf. Rom 3:20; Gal 3:19) — the law reveals the curse before it reveals the path to blessing. The Samaritan Pentateuch reads Gerizim here (a textual variant driven by Samaritan interests in their sacred mountain).
6: avanim shelemot tibne et-mizbach YHWH Eloheicha veha'alita alav olot l'YHWH Eloheicha (ʾăb̠ānîm šĕlēmôt tib̠nê ʾet-mizbēaḥ Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā wĕhaʿălîtā ʿālāyw ʿōlōt lYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'You shall build the altar of the LORD your God with whole stones, and offer burnt offerings on it to the LORD your God.' Avanim shelemot (whole stones, complete/sound stones): the same principle as v5's prohibition of iron tools — the altar must be built with natural, unworked stones. Shalem (whole, complete, at peace) shares a root with shalom; the altar's wholeness mirrors the peace it facilitates. Olot (burnt offerings, whole offerings): the olah is entirely consumed on the altar — the first offering on the Ebal altar is one of total consecration.
7: vezavachta shelamim ve'achalta sham vesimachta lifnei YHWH Eloheicha (wĕzāb̠aḥtā šĕlāmîm wĕʾāḵaltā šām wĕśāmaḥtā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'And you shall offer peace offerings and shall eat there, and you shall rejoice before the LORD your God.' Shelamim (peace offerings, shared offerings): unlike the olah (entirely burned), the shelamim are shared between the altar (fat portions), the priests, and the worshipper's household — a communion meal in divine presence. Vesimachta (you shall rejoice): on the mountain of curses, after the law is inscribed and the offerings made, covenant joy is commanded. The sequence — law inscription → whole burnt offering → shared communion meal → joy — is the covenant pattern: accountability, consecration, reconciliation, celebration.
8: uchtavta al-ha'avanim et kol-divrei hatorah hazot be'er heitiv (ûḵtab̠tā ʿal-hāʾăb̠ānîm ʾet kol-dibrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt b̠āʾēr hêṭēb̠): 'And you shall write on the stones all the words of this law very plainly.' Be'er heitiv (very plainly, making it plain and clear): be'er from ba'ar — to explain, to make clear, to engrave. The Septuagint translates saphōs (clearly). The word deuteronomion in the Septuagint's name for this book comes from a related phrase in 17:18 (mishneh hatorah hazot — a copy/repetition of this law); here the law is written plainly so that all who pass may read it without priestly mediation. The publicness of the Torah is a mark of its covenant character.
9: vayedaber Moshe vehakohanim haLevi'im el-kol-Yisrael lemor hasket ushma Yisrael hayom hazeh nihyeita le'am l'YHWH Eloheicha (waydab̠bēr mōšê wĕhakkōhănîm hălĕwiyyim ʾel-kol-yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr hasqēt ûšmaʿ yiśrāʾēl hayyôm hazzê nihyêtā lĕʿam lYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'Then Moses and the Levitical priests said to all Israel, Keep silence and hear, O Israel: this day you have become the people of the LORD your God.' Hasket (keep silence, be still, pay attention): a strong command for attentive listening. Nihyeita le'am (you have become a people): the covenant constitution is being publicly ratified — in this ceremonial moment, the covenant relationship is formally enacted. The Levitical priests speak alongside Moses, signaling the priestly mediation of Torah that will continue after Moses' death.
10: ushama'ta beqol YHWH Eloheicha ve'asita et-mitzvotav ve'et-chuqqav asher anochi metzavecha hayom (ûšāmaʿtā b̠ĕqôl Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā wĕʿāśîtā ʾet-miṣwōtāyw wĕʾet-ḥuqqāyw ʾăšer ʾānōḵî mĕṣawwĕkā hayyôm): 'You shall therefore obey the voice of the LORD your God, keeping his commandments and his statutes, which I command you today.' Shama' beqol (hear, obey the voice): shema with the preposition be — to listen into, to act upon hearing. The Shema pattern is repeated: hearing is the mode of covenant relationship; obedience is the content of hearing. The verse functions as the response to the proclamation of v9 — the newly constituted people's obligation immediately follows their constitution.
11: vayetzav Moshe et-ha'am bayom hahu lemor (wayyĕṣaw mōšê ʾet-hāʿām bayyôm hahûʾ lēʾmōr): 'And Moses commanded the people on that day, saying.' A brief hinge verse introducing the tribal assignment for the Gerizim-Ebal ceremony. Bayom hahu (on that day): the ceremony is tied to the specific day of crossing — the ratification of the covenant in Canaan is not deferred. Moses' authoritative command allocates the tribes to their positions, ensuring the ceremony's order is divinely specified rather than improvised.
12: eleh ya'amdu levarech et-ha'am al-har Gerizim be'ovretchem et-haYarden Shimon veLevi viYhudah veYissachar veYosef uvBinyamin (ʾēllê yaʿamdû lĕb̠ārēk ʾet-hāʿām ʿal-har gĕrizzîm b̠ĕʿob̠rĕḵem ʾet-hayyardēn šimʿôn wĕlēwî wîhûdâ wĕyiśśāḵār wĕyôsēp̄ ûb̠inyāmîn): 'These shall stand on mount Gerizim to bless the people when you have crossed the Jordan: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.' Six tribes on Gerizim (blessing): note that both Levi and Joseph (Ephraim/Manasseh) are among the blessing-tribes — Levi here as the priestly mediator, Joseph as the double-portioned heir. The assignment is not by geographic proximity or birth order — it follows a theological logic that later commentators have debated (e.g., the Rachel-and-Leah tribal groupings).
13: ve'eleh ya'amdu al-haqelalah behar Eival Reuven Gad ve'Asher uZevulun Dan veNaftali (wĕʾēllê yaʿamdû ʿal-haqqĕlālâ b̠ĕhar ʿêb̠āl rĕʾûb̠ēn gāḏ wĕʾāšēr ûzĕb̠ûlun dān wĕnap̄tālî): 'And these shall stand for the curse on mount Ebal: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.' Six tribes on Ebal (curse). Reuben stands on the curse-side despite being the firstborn — his disqualification (Gen 35:22; 49:3-4) shadows him even in the ceremony. Al-haqelalah (for the curse, over the curse): the tribes do not themselves curse but stand as witnesses and responders to the curses proclaimed by the Levites below (vv. 14-26). The geographic staging — twin mountains facing each other — dramatizes the covenant's binary: blessing or curse, obedience or disobedience.
14: ve'anu haLevi'im ve'amru el-kol-ish Yisrael qol ram (wĕʿānû hallĕwiyyim wĕʾāmrû ʾel-kol-ʾîš yiśrāʾēl qôl rām): 'And the Levites shall declare to all the men of Israel with a loud voice.' Qol ram (a high/loud voice): the curses are proclaimed audibly, publicly, across the valley between the two mountains — the volume ensures that all Israel hears and cannot claim ignorance. The Levites' mediating role (cf. v9) is here performative: they enact the covenant ceremony by declaration. The use of qol ram anticipates the Mishnaic description of the ceremony as a liturgical responsory (m. Sotah 7:5).
15: arur ha'ish asher ya'aseh pesel umasechah to'avat YHWH ma'aseh yedei charash veSam basater ve'anu kol-ha'am ve'amru Amen (ʾārûr hāʾîš ʾăšer yaʿăśê p̄esel ûmasēḵâ tôʿăb̠at Yhwh maʿăśê yĕdê ḥārāš wĕśām bassēter wĕʿānû kol-hāʿām wĕʾāmrû ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be the man who makes a carved or cast metal image, an abomination to the LORD, a thing made by the hands of a craftsman, and sets it up in secret. And all the people shall answer and say, Amen.' The dodecalogue of curses (vv. 15-26) begins with idolatry — the foundational covenant violation. Arur (cursed): a performative declaration of exclusion from covenant blessing. Basater (in secret): the curse specifically targets hidden idolatry — public apostasy could be addressed by courts, but the arur curses penetrate what human courts cannot see. The communal Amen is the congregation's self-imprecation: each person accepts the curse as applicable to themselves should they commit this act.
16: arur meqalel aviv ve'imo ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr mĕqallēl ʾāb̠îw wĕʾimmô wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who dishonors his father or his mother. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Meqalel (dishonors, treats lightly, curses): from qalal — to be light, to treat as worthless, the opposite of kaved (to honor, to make heavy/weighty — cf. the Fifth Commandment, Deut 5:16). The curse again targets the hidden act: public dishonoring of parents could be prosecuted under the law (21:18-21), but the arur addresses contempt that is performed in secret or through subtle patterns of neglect.
17: arur masig gevul re'ehu ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr massîg gĕb̠ûl rēʿēhû wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who moves his neighbor's landmark. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Masig gevul (moves a boundary marker, encroaches on a landmark): gevul is a property boundary — the stone markers that defined land allotments in Israel. Moving them was a secret act of theft that also violated the covenant's land theology: the land was YHWH's gift allocated to each family by lot; encroaching on a neighbor's allotment was encroaching on what YHWH had given them. Cf. Prov 22:28; 23:10; Job 24:2.
18: arur mashgeh iver baderech ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr mašgê ʿiwwēr b̠addāreḵ wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who misleads a blind person on the road. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Mashgeh iver (makes the blind wander/go astray): from shagah — to lead astray, to cause to err. The blind person's inability to verify the directions given makes them wholly dependent on the guide's honesty. Leviticus 19:14 prohibits this in positive form: 'you shall not put a stumbling block before the blind.' The vulnerability of the disabled creates an obligation of care that the law enforces through the covenant curse.
19: arur mateh mishpat ger yatom ve'almanah ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr maṭṭê mišpaṭ gēr yātôm wĕʾalmānâ wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who perverts the justice due to the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Mateh mishpat (perverts justice, bends the judgment): natah in the Hiphil — to cause to turn aside, to deflect what is straight. The three parties again: ger, yatom, almanah — those without legal advocates in a patriarchal society. Judicial perversion of their cases would be done secretly (bribed judges, false witnesses) — making it a prime candidate for the arur curses. The prophets return repeatedly to this violation as evidence of covenant failure (Isa 1:17, 23; Jer 7:6; Zech 7:10).
20: arur shochev im-eshet aviv ki gilah kenaf aviv ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr šōḵēb̠ ʿim-ʾēšet ʾāb̠îw kî gillâ kĕnap̄ ʾāb̠îw wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who lies with his father's wife, because he has uncovered his father's nakedness. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Eshet aviv (his father's wife): includes the stepmother — any wife of the father, not necessarily the man's own mother. Gilah kenaf aviv (he has uncovered his father's skirt/wing): sexual union with a wife is expressed as being under her husband's protective wing (kanaf, cf. Ruth 3:9; Ezek 16:8). To lie with the father's wife is to usurp the father's authority and violate his most intimate boundary — echoing Reuben's disqualification (Gen 35:22; 49:4).
21: arur shochev im-kol-behemah ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr šōḵēb̠ ʿim-kol-bĕhēmâ wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who lies with any kind of animal. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Prohibited in Lev 18:23 and 20:15-16, this act violates the order of creation (species boundaries established at Genesis 1) as well as the covenant code. Its inclusion in the Ebal curses signals its deep violation of the created order that the covenant is designed to uphold. The universality of kol-behemah (any animal) leaves no exceptions.
22: arur shochev im-achoto bat-aviv o vat-imo ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr šōḵēb̠ ʿim-ʾăḥōtô b̠at-ʾāb̠îw ʾô b̠at-ʾimmô wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who lies with his sister, whether the daughter of his father or the daughter of his mother. And all the people shall say, Amen.' The curse covers both full sisters and half-sisters (paternal and maternal) — Abraham's marriage to his half-sister Sarah (Gen 20:12) belongs to the pre-Sinai era; the covenant code established at Sinai (Lev 18:9, 11) prohibits it and the Deuteronomic curse ratifies that prohibition. The double specification (daughter of father or of mother) closes the definitional loophole Abraham's case might suggest.
23: arur shochev im-chamotoh ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr šōḵēb̠ ʿim-ḥămôtô wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who lies with his mother-in-law. And all the people shall say, Amen.' The prohibition in Lev 18:17 and 20:14 covers this: the mother-in-law relationship creates affinity (the covenant of marriage makes the mother of one's spouse a quasi-mother). The violation would typically occur in household proximity — a hidden act that courts could not easily prosecute but the arur formula reaches.
24: arur makeh re'ehu basater ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr makkê rēʿēhû bassēter wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who strikes down his neighbor in secret. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Makeh re'ehu basater (strikes his neighbor secretly): nakah in the Hiphil — to strike down, possibly fatally. Bassater (in secret) is the key qualifier: public killing could be prosecuted by the avenger of blood and the elders; secret assassination evades human justice. The curse invokes YHWH as the ultimate witness to concealed violence — the covenant's eye penetrates where human courts cannot see.
25: arur loqeach shochad lehakot nefesh dam naki ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr lōqēaḥ šōḥad lĕhakkôt nep̄eš dām nāqî wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who takes a bribe to shed innocent blood. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Shochad (bribe): the repeated prohibition of bribery in Deuteronomy (cf. 16:19; 27:25) targets the corruption of the justice system at its point of highest vulnerability — paid witnesses or hired assassins. Dam naki (innocent blood): the blood of the wrongfully killed cries out (Gen 4:10) and defiles the land (Num 35:33-34). The curse against bribe-murder thus protects both covenant justice and the land's purity. Paul's citation of Deut 27:26 (the next verse) in Gal 3:10 implicitly encompasses all these specific curses as evidence of universal human failure under the law.
